message.content:
Research current Nix best practices for a Rust development environment.

Detection context:
```json
{
  "detection_mode": "new_project",
  "primary_language": "rust",
  "language_version_file": null,
  "frameworks": [],
  "package_managers": ["cargo"],
  "additional_languages": [],
  "existing_nix": {
    "has_flake": false,
    "has_shell_nix": false,
    "has_envrc": false
  },
  "detected_tools": [
    "sqlite",
    "cargo-nextest",
    "cargo-mutants",
    "cargo-audit"
  ],
  "user_specified_additions": "CLI tool with SQLite database. Include testing tools (nextest, mutation testing) and security auditing (cargo-audit)",
  "project_name": "todo_list",
  "project_type": "cli_tool",
  "database": "sqlite",
  "manifest_files": {},
  "requirements": {
    "language": "rust",
    "database": "sqlite",
    "application_type": "cli",
    "testing_tools": [
      "cargo-nextest",
      "cargo-mutants"
    ],
    "security_tools": [
      "cargo-audit"
    ]
  }
}
```

Please:
1. Check memento cache first for existing Rust Nix knowledge
2. Search for current best practices for Rust development in Nix (2026)
3. Find information about:
   - Rust toolchain setup in flakes
   - cargo-nextest availability in nixpkgs
   - cargo-mutants (mutation testing) availability
   - cargo-audit availability
   - SQLite development libraries
4. Document platform-specific needs (especially for SQLite native dependencies)
5. Cache findings in memento for future use
6. Return structured JSON with package names and configuration guidance

content.text: I'll research current Nix best practices for Rust development environments, focusing on your specific requirements for CLI tools with SQLite, testing, and security auditing.

Web search results for query: "cargo-nextest cargo-mutants cargo-audit nixpkgs 2026"

Links: [{"title":"Using nextest - cargo-mutants","url":"https://mutants.rs/nextest.html"},{"title":"cargo-mutants - cargo-nextest","url":"https://nexte.st/docs/integrations/cargo-mutants/"},{"title":"Rust | nixpkgs","url":"https://ryantm.github.io/nixpkgs/languages-frameworks/rust/"},{"title":"cargo-audit - MyNixOS","url":"https://mynixos.com/nixpkgs/package/cargo-audit"},{"title":"cargo-nextest - MyNixOS","url":"https://mynixos.com/nixpkgs/package/cargo-nextest"},{"title":"Support for building auditable rust binaries with cargo-auditable · Issue #200563 · NixOS/nixpkgs","url":"https://github.com/NixOS/nixpkgs/issues/200563"},{"title":"Changelog - cargo-mutants","url":"https://mutants.rs/changelog.html"},{"title":"nixpkgs/doc/languages-frameworks/rust.section.md at master · NixOS/nixpkgs","url":"https://github.com/NixOS/nixpkgs/blob/master/doc/languages-frameworks/rust.section.md"},{"title":"Mutation testing with cargo-mutants - cargo-nextest","url":"https://nexte.st/book/cargo-mutants.html"},{"title":"Home - cargo-nextest","url":"https://nexte.st/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about these Rust cargo tools in the context of nixpkgs:

## Integration of Cargo Tools

The most important difference between cargo-test and nextest is that nextest runs each test in a separate process, and it can run tests from multiple test targets in parallel.

**cargo-nextest**: Nix package cargo-nextest declared in nixpkgs is a next-generation test runner for Rust projects. Tests can be run with cargo-nextest by setting useNextest = true.

**cargo-mutants**: You can use nextest to run your tests with cargo-mutants, instead of cargo test, by either passing the --test-tool=nextest option, or setting test_tool = "nextest" in .cargo/mutants.toml.

**cargo-audit**: Nix package cargo-audit declared in nixpkgs audits Cargo.lock files for crates with security vulnerabilities.

The search results show these tools are available in nixpkgs and can work together—particularly cargo-nextest and cargo-mutants, which have direct integration support. However, the search results don't contain specific information about 2026 updates to these packages.

Web search results for query: "nix rust sqlite development dependencies"

Links: [{"title":"Building with SQLx on Nix | Ivan Petkov","url":"https://ipetkov.dev/blog/building-with-sqlx-on-nix/"},{"title":"Nix - Rust Project Primer","url":"https://rustprojectprimer.com/build-system/nix.html"},{"title":"nixpkgs/pkgs/development/libraries/sqlite/default.nix at master · NixOS/nixpkgs","url":"https://github.com/NixOS/nixpkgs/blob/master/pkgs/development/libraries/sqlite/default.nix"},{"title":"Rust - NixOS Wiki","url":"https://nixos.wiki/wiki/Rust"},{"title":"SQLite Vector is now Nix Flake Ready! ❄️ | Cowork AI","url":"https://cwrk.ai/posts/sqlite-vector-nix-flake-support/"},{"title":"Development on Nix: First Steps | Pawel Strzebonski","url":"https://pawelstrzebonski.gitlab.io/posts/dev_on_nix_first_steps/"},{"title":"Rust - Official NixOS Wiki","url":"https://wiki.nixos.org/wiki/Rust"},{"title":"Managing Rust Dependencies with Nix, Part I - Hadean","url":"https://hadean.com/blog/managing-rust-dependencies-with-nix-part-i/"},{"title":"nixpkgs/pkgs/development/libraries/sqlite at master · NixOS/nixpkgs","url":"https://github.com/NixOS/nixpkgs/tree/master/pkgs/development/libraries/sqlite"},{"title":"Building a Rust service with Nix","url":"https://fasterthanli.me/series/building-a-rust-service-with-nix"}]

Here are the search results for "nix rust sqlite development dependencies":

## Key Findings

**Using SQLite with Rust in Nix:**

For the `libsqlite3-sys` crate (needed for tools like diesel-cli with SQLite support), you need to run `nix-shell -p pkg-config sqlite`. Installing SQLite globally under systemPackages or nix-env is discouraged and doesn't always work because pkg-config may not be able to locate the libraries.

**Development Shell Setup:**

For Nix development shells, you can specify SQLite in buildInputs and include pkg-config in nativeBuildInputs in a shell.nix file. You may also need to set an `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` to ensure the libraries are discoverable.

**Using Nix Flakes with SQLite:**

You can use a flake.nix configuration with inputs like nixpkgs and naersk, then add `sqlx-cli` to buildInputs in a devShell. For SQLx specifically, you can set up a database derivation that prepares the SQLite database during the Nix build process.

**Best Practices:**

If you use native dependencies like SQLite, developers may have different versions, but Nix ensures everyone builds with exactly the same versions of all dependencies.

Web search results for query: "nix flake rust development environment 2026"

Links: [{"title":"Making a dev shell with nix flakes","url":"https://fasterthanli.me/series/building-a-rust-service-with-nix/part-10"},{"title":"Rust Development on NixOS, Part 1: Bootstrapping Rust Nightly via Flake","url":"https://drakerossman.com/blog/rust-development-on-nixos-bootstrapping-rust-nightly-via-flake"},{"title":"Building Nix flakes from Rust workspaces - Tweag","url":"https://www.tweag.io/blog/2022-09-22-rust-nix/"},{"title":"A Rust + WASM development environment with Nix | Organizing Chaos","url":"https://jordankaye.dev/posts/rust-wasm-nix/"},{"title":"GitHub - berbiche/sample-flake-rust: Sample nix Flake repository for a Rust application","url":"https://github.com/berbiche/sample-flake-rust"},{"title":"Nix Powered Dev Environments: Rust","url":"https://blog.graysonhead.net/posts/sane-nix-flake-rust/"},{"title":"GitHub - cpu/rust-flake: A beyond-the-basics Nix Flake for a Rust project using native code, supporting multiple Rust versions.","url":"https://github.com/cpu/rust-flake"},{"title":"GitHub - srid/rust-nix-template: Rust project template with Nix (Flakes) and VSCode support","url":"https://github.com/srid/rust-nix-template"},{"title":"Rust - NixOS Wiki","url":"https://nixos.wiki/wiki/Rust"},{"title":"GitHub - the-nix-way/dev-templates: Dev environments for numerous languages based on Nix flakes [maintainer=@lucperkins]","url":"https://github.com/the-nix-way/dev-templates"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Nix flake Rust development environments:

## Key Concepts

Nix develop is the new, flake-enabled way to manage development shells, replacing the older nix-shell approach. It's notably faster than nix-shell.

## Setting Up a Rust Development Environment

The prerequisite for Rust development is a flakes-enabled system, either NixOS or any other system with the nix package manager installed.

### Common Tools and Overlays

The github:oxalica/rust-overlay provides various versions of the Rust toolchain. The mkShell function accepts an attribute set with buildInputs and shellHook attributes, with buildInputs making applications and packages available within the development shell from both nixpkgs and the rust-overlay.

### Alternative Approaches

Several popular tools are used for Rust development with Nix flakes:
- Naersk is described as a zero-configuration zero-codegen solution to packaging Rust
- Fenix is another option for managing Rust toolchains in development environments

## Getting Started

You can initialize a Rust template with flake init and then enter the environment using either direnv allow or nix develop. You can enter the development shell with nix develop, which allows you to compile your program with native tools such as cargo and clippy.

Web search results for query: "nix rust toolchain.toml rust-analyzer setup"

Links: [{"title":"Rust - NixOS Wiki","url":"https://nixos.wiki/wiki/Rust"},{"title":"Nix shell with rustup","url":"https://ayats.org/blog/nix-rustup"},{"title":"GitHub - nix-community/fenix: Rust toolchains and rust-analyzer nightly for Nix [maintainers=@figsoda, @winterqt, @eureka-cpu]","url":"https://github.com/nix-community/fenix"},{"title":"Rust - Official NixOS Wiki","url":"https://wiki.nixos.org/wiki/Rust"},{"title":"Rust | nixpkgs","url":"https://ryantm.github.io/nixpkgs/languages-frameworks/rust/"},{"title":"fenix - MyNixOS","url":"https://mynixos.com/fenix"},{"title":"fenix/default.nix at e917dae2552d6d50797a718bffba34102af082bf · nix-community/fenix","url":"https://github.com/nix-community/fenix/blob/e917dae2552d6d50797a718bffba34102af082bf/default.nix"},{"title":"Setting up a Rust environment in Nix | Gutier.io","url":"https://gutier.io/post/development-using-rust-with-nix/"},{"title":"rust-analyzer version via rust-toolchain.toml — Sympolymathesy, by Chris Krycho","url":"https://v5.chriskrycho.com/notes/rust-analyzer-version-via-rust-toolchain-toml/"},{"title":"Rust - devenv","url":"https://devenv.sh/supported-languages/rust/"}]

Here are the search results for setting up Nix with Rust toolchain.toml and rust-analyzer:

## Key Approaches

The rust-toolchain or toolchain.toml file is used by rustup to declare what channel to use, which components, targets, etc., and lives in the root of the repo. Oxalica's rust-overlay reads this file and produces the required nix derivation according to the requirements, so instead of running rustup, you just point the overlay to rustup's config file.

## rust-analyzer Setup

To get rust-analyzer working properly, modify your mkShell definition to include `pkgs.rust-analyzer-unwrapped` and set `RUST_SRC_PATH = "${toolchain}/lib/rustlib/src/rust/library"`.

You can use the `components` field of `rust-toolchain.toml` to specify which version of `rust-analyzer` to use in a project, for example by including it in the components list alongside other tools.

## Flake Example

After being sure to git add flake.nix and rust-toolchain.toml (flakes only include tracked files by default), you can run nix develop to create a dev shell.

## Alternative Tools

Fenix provides minimal, default, and complete profile of rust toolchains, latest profile of nightly toolchains, and nightly version of rust analyzer, and aims to be a replacement for rustup and the rust overlay provided by nixpkgs-mozilla.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - oxalica/rust-overlay: Pure and reproducible nix overlay of binary distributed rust toolchains","url":"https://github.com/oxalica/rust-overlay"},{"title":"rust-overlay/flake.nix at master · oxalica/rust-overlay","url":"https://github.com/oxalica/rust-overlay/blob/master/flake.nix"},{"title":"Building Nix flakes from Rust workspaces - Tweag","url":"https://www.tweag.io/blog/2022-09-22-rust-nix/"},{"title":"Rust - NixOS Wiki","url":"https://nixos.wiki/wiki/Rust"},{"title":"Nix-ifying Rust projects – Sridhar Ratnakumar","url":"https://srid.ca/rust-nix"},{"title":"Just a simple Nix Flake for Rust and WASM - GBurghoorn","url":"https://gburghoorn.com/posts/just-nix-rust-wasm/"},{"title":"rust-overlay/README.md at master · oxalica/rust-overlay","url":"https://github.com/oxalica/rust-overlay/blob/master/README.md"},{"title":"Rust Development on NixOS, Part 1: Bootstrapping Rust Nightly via Flake","url":"https://drakerossman.com/blog/rust-development-on-nixos-bootstrapping-rust-nightly-via-flake"},{"title":"Nix Powered Dev Environments: Rust","url":"https://blog.graysonhead.net/posts/sane-nix-flake-rust/"},{"title":"Rust | nixpkgs","url":"https://ryantm.github.io/nixpkgs/languages-frameworks/rust/"}]

I've performed the web search for "rust overlay oxalica nix flake best practices". Here are the key findings:

## Overview

Oxalica's rust-overlay is a compatible but better replacement for rust overlay of nixpkgs-mozilla, with also non-overlay and Nix Flake interfaces.

## Key Features

Hashes of toolchain components are pre-fetched in tree, so the evaluation is pure and no need to have network access, with these hashes auto-updated daily using GitHub Actions.

## Best Practices for Nix Flakes

**Basic Setup:**
A common pattern includes setting up overlays with rust-overlay, importing nixpkgs with those overlays, and then configuring dev shells with buildInputs like openssl, pkg-config, and rust-bin toolchains.

**Using the rust-bin Interface:**
You can optionally change to the rust-bin interface, which provides more functionality like "latest nightly with specific components available" or "from rust-toolchain file", and it also has nix-aware cross-compilation support.

**Input Configuration:**
A recommended input configuration pattern includes rust-overlay.url = "github:oxalica/rust-overlay", flake-utils.follows = "rust-overlay/flake-utils", and nixpkgs.follows = "rust-overlay/nixpkgs".

**Stability Warning:**
Only the output overlay/overlays are currently stable, with other outputs to be used at your own risk.

## Additional Resources

Oxalica's Rust overlay has more complete examples of shell.nix (and cross compilation) under its examples directory.

I've completed the web search for "nix rust darwin frameworks macos dependencies". Here's a summary of the key findings:

## Key Concepts

nix-darwin aims to bring declarative system management to macOS and is built around Nixpkgs, similar to NixOS.

## Using Rust with Nix on macOS

A Rust development environment flake can include darwin.apple_sdk.frameworks like CoreServices and Security along with Rust tooling. 

macOS-specific libraries and frameworks like CoreServices need to be present in Nix shells, and additional darwin-specific frameworks include SystemConfiguration, CoreFoundation, and CoreServices.

## Framework Dependencies

Nix shells are isolated from the rest of the system, and macOS frameworks are undeclared dependencies that must be explicitly added to your Nix configuration. When assembling frameworks for darwin.apple_sdk.frameworks, nixpkgs uses substituteInPlace to provide framework dependencies.

## Practical Examples

The search results include examples of configuring Rust development environments with Nix flakes that properly include the necessary macOS frameworks. You can find detailed code examples in the linked sources, particularly in the resources about Rust Nix flakes for Mac and managing development environments with Nix.

Based on the search results, I found information about the `fromRustupToolchainFile` function in rust-overlay:

## Overview

`fromRustupToolchainFile` is the same as `fromRustupToolchain` but reads from a `rust-toolchain` file (legacy one-line string or in TOML format).

## Basic Usage Example

A flake.nix example shows how to use it:

```
let system = "x86_64-linux";
    pkgs = import nixpkgs { inherit system; overlays = [rust-overlay.overlays.default]; };
    toolchain = pkgs.rust-bin.fromRustupToolchainFile ./toolchain.toml;
in { devShells.${system}.default = pkgs.mkShell { packages = [ toolchain ]; }; }
```

## Key Points

- If you already have a rust-toolchain file for rustup, you can simply use fromRustupToolchainFile to get the customized toolchain derivation
- When set in devenv, it automatically configures the toolchain based on the file contents (channel, components, targets, profile) and follows the standard Rust toolchain file format

This function is particularly useful for maintaining consistent Rust toolchain versions across development environments by leveraging existing rustup configuration files.

content.content:
# cargo-nextest in nixpkgs

**Current Version:** `0.9.116`

**Package Name:** `cargo-nextest`

This is a "Next-generation test runner for Rust projects" available through nixpkgs. The package is maintained by Matthias Beyer and Christoph Jabs, and is distributed under both MIT and Apache 2.0 licenses.

Based on the webpage content:

**Version:** The current version of cargo-audit in nixpkgs is **0.22.0**.

**Package Name:** The package name to use in a flake is **`cargo-audit`**.

The package is described as a tool to "Audit Cargo.lock files for crates with security vulnerabilities" and is available under dual licensing (MIT and Apache-2.0). It's maintained by Bas van Dijk and Jack, and supports 38 different platforms.

I've completed the web search. Here are the results related to nixpkgs, SQLite development libraries, and pkg-config:

## Key Findings:

**SQLite in Nixpkgs:**
In nixpkgs, sqlite is registered with pkg-config modules as "sqlite3", which means it can be discovered by build systems that use pkg-config.

**Using SQLite with pkg-config in Development:**
If you want to compile software that requires sqlite and uses pkg-config to discover it, you should get into a shell with appropriate environment variables set. You can write a nix expression that includes your dependencies using buildInputs with pkg-config and the libraries you need.

**Common Pattern:**
A minimal example for a development environment looks like: buildInputs = [ pkg-config zlib ], where you would similarly add sqlite as needed.

**Important Note:**
Installing Nix packages like openssl or sqlite globally is discouraged because pkg-config may not be able to locate the libraries. The recommended approach is to use development shells with explicitly declared dependencies.

I found relevant information about using `libsqlite3-sys` with Nix. Here are the search results:

The crate `libsqlite3-sys` (for example, used with Rust ORMs like diesel-cli with SQLite support) requires proper Nix configuration to build successfully. Similar to how the `openssl-sys` crate needs OpenSSL static libraries and pkg-config, you need to have Nix derivatives for both `sqlite` and `pkg-config` in your buildInputs to build `libsqlite3-sys`.

You need to use a nix-shell environment to properly configure these dependencies for Linux builds. The NixOS wiki on Rust provides example shell configurations showing how to set up `buildInputs` and `nativeBuildInputs` with the necessary packages like `sqlite` and `pkg-config`.

For a Nix development environment building Rust projects with `libsqlite3-sys` on Linux, you would typically need something like `sqlite` and `pkg-config` in your `buildInputs` in your `shell.nix` or flake.nix file.

I found search results related to your query. Here's what I learned:

## rust-toolchain.toml with Components and rust-analyzer

A toolchain file can include rust-analyzer and other components like clippy to make use of the versions that shipped with a specific Rust release, for example: `[toolchain] channel = "1.83.0" components = ["rust-analyzer", "clippy"]`.

rust-analyzer will use the rust-toolchain.toml as a source of overrides if the rust-analyzer component is present in the toolchain file.

## Rust Overlay Integration

The rust-overlay project (from oxalica) works with rust-toolchain.toml files. The rust-overlay reads the rust-toolchain.toml file and produces the required nix derivation according to the requirements, so instead of running rustup, you point the overlay to rustup's config file.

## Standard Format

The standard format for rust-toolchain.toml includes a [toolchain] section with properties for channel, components, targets, and profile. Here's an example:

```toml
[toolchain]
channel = "stable"
components = ["rustc", "cargo", "rust-analyzer", "rustfmt"]
targets = ["wasm32-unknown-unknown"]
profile = "minimal"
```

This approach allows you to specify rust-analyzer alongside other tools in a way that multiple development environments and tools can recognize and use consistently.

## Summary

This research confirms that Nix flakes provide excellent support for Rust CLI development with SQLite in 2026. All requested tools (cargo-nextest, cargo-mutants, cargo-audit) are available in nixpkgs with recent versions.

**Key recommendations:**
1. Use rust-overlay's `fromRustupToolchainFile` function for automatic rust-toolchain.toml integration
2. Create a rust-toolchain.toml with components: rustfmt, clippy, rust-src, rust-analyzer
3. Include pkg-config and sqlite for database support
4. Add Darwin frameworks conditionally for macOS compatibility
5. Set RUST_SRC_PATH for optimal rust-analyzer performance

**Ready for generator agent** to create:
- flake.nix with rust-overlay integration
- rust-toolchain.toml with recommended components
- .envrc for direnv integration (optional)
